data.title: Procore Technologies: Remain Buy Rated On Growth Acceleration Beyond FY25
data.description:
Investment summary

My previous investment thought on Procore Technologies (NYSE:PCOR) (published on 5th July 2024) was a buy rating (one that has worked very well, with share price up 30% since) because of the structural tailwind that PCOR enjoys. PCOR continues to execute well, as seen from the strong 4Q24 results. In particular, PCOR's sales strategy transition continues to gain momentum, which, I believe, will enable PCOR to see revenue growth acceleration in FY26.

4Q24 results update

Released last week, PCOR reported 4Q24 revenue of $302 million, reflecting a 16% y/y growth, slightly ahead of consensus estimates of $298 million. The more positive takeaway was PCOR cRPO (current remaining performance obligations), which grew 19% y/y, an acceleration from the 16% seen in 2/3Q24. Even if we were to adjust for early renewals that contributed 3–4 points to cRPO, underlying growth was still in the mid-teens. In addition, PCOR's ability to penetrate the upmarket is notable in this quarter: customers with >$1 million in annual recurring revenue [ARR] grew 39% y/y. On the profitability side, PCOR expanded adj EBIT margin by 800 bps on a full-year basis (from 2% in FY23 to 10% in FY24), which is notable because FY24 was a year of investments-in sales transformation and AI-driven platform enhancements. Looking ahead, management guided adj EBIT margin expansion of another 300-350 basis points.

GTM Transition Gaining Traction and Driving Enterprise Expansion

I remain bullish about PCOR's growth outlook. PCOR has made very good progress in its GTM transition. It was very encouraging to hear that PCOR has completed its sales force hiring targets ahead of schedule because this sets the stage for PCOR's P&L to "show" the ramp-up in sales productivity (i.e., without new hires, PCOR can focus on pipeline conversion). Also, we will finally see the impact from this shift from a generalist sales model to a specialist-driven model, which should allow PCOR to better address the needs of different customer segments, particularly as it scales its enterprise business (note: large enterprises have much more complex and customized needs). In fact, the impact of this shift is already becoming evident. In 4Q24, PCOR won multiple seven-figure ARR contracts, including one of the largest deals in company history, and it also managed to expand its deals with existing customers (including top-tier general contractors and real estate owners).

The bigger implication from this shift is also that PCOR is now able to service more complex and multi-stakeholder projects. This makes PCOR a lot more intertwined into the underlying workflow processes, which means it becomes much stickier. In other words, it is a lot harder for competitors like Autodesk (ADSK) (for instance) to displace it. Customers cannot simply rip out PCOR and replace it with ADSK in the middle of the project because so many resources, time, and knowledge (of the project) have been invested (loaded) into PCOR's platform. Of

As such, looking ahead, I see the GTM transition as a key driver of PCOR's next leg of growth. As sales reps become more mature and specialized, and PCOR becomes better at developing the right customer engagement strategy (the "pitch"), I expect PCOR to improve win rates, attachment rates, and retention rates. So long as PCOR can continue to execute, FY26 should be the year of growth inflection (back to >20% y/y).

Pay remains as a growth catalyst

PCOR pay remains one of the biggest growth catalysts that could drive serious acceleration in growth. As a recap, Procore Pay enables automated invoicing and payments within the platform. So far, it has already onboarded >250 customers, which is compelling proof that the product works. As I have said before, the construction industry's payment process is very complex, and PCOR is well positioned to facilitate this process, given it is the platform that clients and stakeholders use. Which means, PCOR can effectively eliminate all manual processing and ensure that subcontractors and vendors are paid more efficiently (i.e., on time). The good news is that Procore Pay is not embedded into FY25 guidance (management does not expect it to materially impact FY25 results), so if PCOR sees better than expected traction, it could potentially lead to a massive beat vs. guidance and consensus estimates.

Below, I showcased my math again, but updated for FY25 guidance:

To illustrate the potential monetization opportunity, for every $1 of construction volume, PCOR is able to capture $0.85 of that spend. Assuming that the PCOR take rate is similar to that of a prominent payment processor (using Adyen as a benchmark), it should be able to capture ~20bps of that $0.85 spend. PCOR currently captures about $557 billion of construction spend so far, based on the figures provided above, and this translates to $1.11 billion of revenue opportunity if PCOR can process all of this volume.

Valuation

Despite a solid share price rally so far, I still see upsides to PCOR share price. Previously, I thought that PCOR should trade at a discount to ADSK because it has a similar growth profile but is less profitable. However, I no longer think this is the case moving forward, as PCOR has made very encouraging progress in its go-to-market strategy shift, which instilled confidence that growth can accelerate higher from mid-teens level. If this plays out as I expected, PCOR revenue growth could recover to >20% (from FY25 guided 12%), which is a premium vs. ADSK's mid-teens growth outlook. Just on this point itself, it can be argued that PCOR should trade at a premium to ADSK. However, like I said previously, ADSK is a much more profitable business vs. PCOR, and as such, this justifies PCOR trading at a discount to ADSK. Netting both positive and negative against each other, I would think it makes sense for PCOR to minimally trade in line with the ADSK valuation multiple (ADSK trades at 9.5x). In my model, I expect PCOR to accelerate growth back to 21%, and attaching a 9.5x multiple to my FY27 revenue estimate of $1.9 billion, it equates to an upside of 44%.

Risk

While the growth outlook is attractive, I would point out some mini red flags that may indicate potential growth weakness. For instance, new customer additions slowed to 113 in 4Q24, marking the lowest since going public, with customer growth decelerating to 4% y/y. Net revenue retention [NRR] also declined to 106% from 114% in FY23, reflecting weaker expansion trends. If these metrics worsen, it could lead to growth decelerating to much lower levels, thereby impacting the growth acceleration narrative.

Conclusion

My view for PCOR remains a buy rating. PCOR GTM transition efforts are ongoing very well, evident from improving enterprise penetration and large contract wins. Meanwhile, Procore Pay represents a strong growth catalyst that is not embedded into market estimates. Valuation-wise, I think PCOR should trade at least at the same level vs. ADSK, and this should translate into a 44% upside to the current share price.

data.title: Understanding The Fluctuations Of China Share Prices: Insights And Trends For Investors
data.description:
China's stock market is a complex and ever-changing landscape, full of opportunities and challenges for investors. Whether you're a seasoned trader or just starting out, understanding the factors that influence share prices is key to making informed decisions. This article breaks down the drivers behind daily price swings, the role of government policies, currency impacts, and more. Let's dive into what shapes the ups and downs of China share prices.

Key Drivers Behind Daily Price Momentum in China Share Prices

Role of Retail Investors in Market Fluctuations

Retail investors play a significant role in shaping the daily price momentum of Chinese stocks. Unlike institutional investors, retail participants often rely on emotional decision-making and short-term trends, which can amplify market volatility. These investors frequently engage in speculative trading, leading to rapid price changes that may not align with the underlying fundamentals of the stocks.

  • Retail investors dominate daily trading volumes in China's stock markets, accounting for a substantial share of transactions.
  • Their tendency to chase trending stocks often creates momentum that persists for a day or two.
  • This behavior can lead to sharp reversals when the momentum fades, especially as professional traders capitalize on these movements.

Understanding the behavior of retail investors is essential for predicting short-term price trends in Chinese equities.

Impact of Cognitive Biases on Stock Movements

Cognitive biases significantly influence the decisions of both retail and some institutional investors. Herd behavior, for instance, drives many to follow market trends without conducting thorough analysis. This can lead to overreactions, where stocks are either overbought or oversold within a short timeframe.

Key biases affecting momentum include:

  1. Confirmation bias: Investors favor information that aligns with their existing beliefs, ignoring contradictory data.
  2. Overconfidence: Many traders overestimate their ability to predict market movements, resulting in aggressive trades.
  3. Loss aversion: Fear of losses often leads to panic selling during market downturns, exacerbating price swings.

Short-Term Trends and Reversals in Chinese Markets

Daily price momentum in Chinese stock markets is unique compared to other global markets. Stocks that perform well on one day often continue their trajectory the next day before reversing within a week. This pattern contrasts with the medium- and long-term momentum seen in other major markets.

Timeframe Typical Trend in Chinese Markets Comparison to Global Markets
1-2 Days Strong momentum Moderate momentum
3-7 Days Reversal phase Continuation of trends
1 Month or Longer Limited predictive momentum Medium- to long-term trends

This short-term nature of momentum is influenced by high trading volumes, speculative activities, and the rapid dissemination of news in China's stock market. For investors, this means opportunities for quick gains but also heightened risks of sudden reversals.

The Influence of Economic Policies on China Share Prices

Recent Policy Shifts and Their Market Impacts

China's policymakers have been increasingly active in shaping economic outcomes, especially in response to slowing growth and external pressures. Recent measures include monetary easing, fiscal stimulus, and regulatory reforms, all aimed at stabilizing the economy and boosting investor confidence. These policies have led to some of the strongest equity market rallies in years, surprising many investors with their timing and magnitude.

Some key policy actions include:

  • Lowering interest rates to encourage borrowing and investment.
  • Reducing reserve requirements for banks to enhance liquidity.
  • Introducing fiscal initiatives, such as subsidies and tax breaks, to stimulate consumption and industrial output.

While these measures have sparked optimism, their long-term effectiveness in driving sustainable growth remains uncertain, requiring close monitoring by investors.

Monetary Easing and Fiscal Stimulus Effects

China's monetary policy has become one of the most accommodative among major global economies. The People's Bank of China (PBOC) has reduced key rates multiple times, with the 10-year government bond yield dropping to historic lows of 1.82% in late 2024. This low-yield environment has made equities more attractive compared to bonds, spurring increased market activity.

Key fiscal efforts include:

  1. Expanding infrastructure projects to create jobs and drive economic activity.
  2. Offering subsidies to emerging industries, such as green energy and technology.
  3. Supporting local governments with additional funding to manage debt and finance regional development.

However, challenges such as sluggish credit growth and weak consumer confidence persist, highlighting the need for more targeted interventions.

Investor Sentiment in Response to Policy Changes

Investor sentiment has been a critical factor in the market's response to these policies. The recent announcements have reduced fears of economic stagnation and deflation, leading to a more neutral or even optimistic outlook on Chinese equities. For instance, the equity risk premium - measuring the attractiveness of stocks over bonds - has reached levels that typically precede bull runs.

Factors influencing sentiment include:

  • Clear government commitment to market stability.
  • Reduced concerns over currency depreciation.
  • A perceived shift away from piecemeal to more coordinated policy efforts.

Investors, particularly those with limited exposure to China, are beginning to see tactical opportunities in the market. However, uncertainties around policy implementation and global economic conditions continue to weigh on decision-making.

Currency Dynamics and Their Effect on China Share Prices

Stability and Strength of the Yuan

The value of the Chinese yuan (CNY) plays a pivotal role in shaping the performance of China's stock market. A stronger yuan typically attracts foreign investors, as it enhances returns when converted back to their home currencies. However, when the yuan weakens, it often leads to capital outflows, putting downward pressure on share prices. The People's Bank of China frequently intervenes in the foreign exchange market to stabilize the yuan, especially during periods of heightened volatility.

In recent years, the yuan has faced dual challenges:

  • Negative carry compared to other major currencies, discouraging foreign inflows.
  • Geopolitical tensions that amplify currency risks and uncertainty.

Geopolitical Factors Influencing Currency Trends

Geopolitical developments, such as trade tensions or shifting alliances, significantly impact the yuan's valuation. For instance, tariff disputes with major trading partners or sanctions can weaken the yuan, making Chinese exports more competitive but simultaneously eroding investor confidence. Additionally, global events like U.S. elections or changes in Federal Reserve policies can ripple through to China's currency and, by extension, its stock market.

Key geopolitical influences include:

  1. Trade agreements or disputes that alter export dynamics.
  2. Sanctions or restrictions on Chinese companies operating abroad.
  3. Shifts in global monetary policies that affect capital flows.

Implications of Currency Movements for Investors

For investors, understanding the interplay between currency movements and stock prices is essential. A depreciating yuan can benefit export-driven sectors, such as manufacturing and technology, by making their products cheaper internationally. Conversely, sectors reliant on imports, like consumer goods, may face margin pressures due to higher costs.

Currency Movement Impact on Sectors
Yuan Appreciation Boosts consumer goods, lowers exports
Yuan Depreciation Supports exporters, raises import costs

Currency stability is not just about exchange rates; it's a mirror reflecting broader economic and political conditions.

Investors should monitor both domestic policies and external geopolitical factors to navigate these dynamics effectively. A balanced portfolio that considers currency-sensitive sectors can help mitigate risks while capitalizing on opportunities presented by yuan fluctuations.

Sectoral Trends and Opportunities in the Chinese Stock Market

Emerging Technologies and Their Market Impact

China is rapidly becoming a global leader in emerging technologies, with sectors like artificial intelligence (AI), electric vehicles (EVs), and renewable energy witnessing significant growth. Investors are closely watching these industries as they represent China's push toward innovation and self-reliance in critical technologies. For example:

  • The EV sector has seen companies like BYD and NIO expand their market share both domestically and internationally.
  • AI advancements are being driven by firms such as Baidu and Tencent, who are heavily investing in generative AI models.
  • Renewable energy companies are benefiting from government subsidies focused on solar panel and wind turbine production.

These industries not only offer growth potential but also align with China's long-term strategic goals, making them attractive for investors aiming to capitalize on future trends.

Consumer and Internet Sectors as Growth Drivers

The consumer sector, particularly e-commerce and online services, continues to thrive in China. Companies like Alibaba and JD.com have built expansive ecosystems that cater to an increasingly digital-savvy population. Meanwhile, the internet sector is evolving with:

  1. Increased adoption of cloud computing services.
  2. Expansion of digital payment platforms like Alipay and WeChat Pay.
  3. Growth in online entertainment, including gaming and streaming services.

These trends are underpinned by a growing middle class and rising disposable incomes, which fuel demand for convenience and connectivity. However, regulatory scrutiny remains a wildcard, influencing investor sentiment.

Industrials and Their Role in Market Performance

Industrials remain a cornerstone of China's economy, contributing significantly to the stock market's performance. This sector includes construction, manufacturing, and infrastructure development. Key factors driving the industrial sector include:

  • Government-led infrastructure projects aimed at economic stimulus.
  • Export-oriented manufacturing supported by a stable yuan.
  • Transition to high-value manufacturing, such as robotics and advanced machinery.

While the industrial sector is less glamorous than tech or consumer industries, its stability and consistent demand make it a reliable choice for long-term investors. Additionally, industrial firms often benefit from policy support during economic downturns, providing a cushion against market volatility.

China's stock market offers a diverse range of opportunities across sectors, but understanding the unique dynamics of each is essential for making informed investment decisions.

Comparative Analysis: China Share Prices Versus Global Markets

Equity Risk Premiums in China and the US

The equity risk premium (ERP) serves as a key metric for evaluating stock market attractiveness. In China, the ERP has recently hit decade highs, driven by a combination of low 10-year government bond yields and relatively high dividend yields. This contrasts sharply with the United States, where the ERP is nearing decade lows as equity markets soar to record levels. This divergence highlights the potential for higher returns in Chinese equities, especially during periods of accommodative monetary policy.

To illustrate:

Metric China (2024) US (2024)
10-Year Bond Yield 1.82% ~4.0%
Average Dividend Yield ~3.5% ~1.5%
Equity Risk Premium High Low

Investors in China are responding to this opportunity, as evidenced by a rise in margin collateral usage for leveraged investments.

Performance of Chinese Equities Relative to GDP

Historically, Chinese equities have struggled to keep pace with the country's GDP growth. For example, while China's GDP grew more than sixfold between 2006 and 2024, the MSCI China Index showed only modest gains during the same period. This disconnect often stems from structural factors, such as market inefficiencies and investor sentiment, which can suppress equity performance despite robust economic fundamentals.

Key observations:

  • GDP growth does not always translate into proportional stock market returns.
  • Market reforms and policy shifts may help bridge this gap over time.
  • Understanding this dynamic is critical for long-term investors.

Lessons from Historical Market Trends

Looking back at historical trends, Chinese equities tend to perform well during periods of strong policy support and favorable global conditions. For instance, recent easing measures in late 2024 led to one of the best market rallies in years. However, geopolitical tensions and economic uncertainties often act as counterweights, limiting sustained growth.

Investors should weigh the interplay between domestic policy shifts and external factors when evaluating potential returns in Chinese markets.

In summary, while Chinese equities offer unique opportunities, they also come with distinct challenges. By analyzing metrics like the ERP and understanding historical patterns, investors can make more informed decisions when comparing China to global markets.

Challenges and Risks in Investing in China Share Prices

Deflationary Pressures and Economic Uncertainty

China's economy has been grappling with deflationary pressures, a phenomenon that can significantly impact stock market performance. Deflation reduces corporate revenues and profits, making equities less appealing to investors. Compounding this is the uncertainty surrounding consumer spending, as households remain cautious despite government efforts to stimulate demand. For investors, the unpredictable nature of these economic conditions adds a layer of complexity to decision-making.

Key challenges include:

  • Persistent deflationary trends impacting corporate earnings.
  • High household savings rates, leading to subdued consumer spending.
  • Uncertainty around government policy effectiveness in reversing deflation.

Investors need to weigh the risks of a prolonged deflationary environment against potential policy-driven market rebounds.

Impact of Geopolitical Tensions on Market Stability

Geopolitical factors play an outsized role in influencing China's stock market. Trade disputes, sanctions, and diplomatic tensions can lead to sudden market volatility. For instance, renewed tariff discussions or restrictions on technology exports can disrupt investor sentiment and sectoral performance, particularly in tech and industrials.

Considerations for investors:

  1. Monitor developments in U.S.-China relations, as these often have immediate market implications.
  2. Assess sector-specific risks, especially in industries exposed to geopolitical scrutiny, such as semiconductors.
  3. Diversify investments to mitigate the impact of region-specific tensions.

Navigating Volatility in Emerging Markets

Emerging markets, including China, are inherently volatile due to their reliance on external factors like global commodity prices and currency stability. The Chinese yuan's fluctuations, for example, can directly affect the profitability of export-driven companies. Moreover, the dominance of retail investors in China's market often amplifies short-term volatility, as emotional trading leads to rapid price swings.

To manage this volatility, investors might:

  • Focus on long-term fundamentals rather than short-term market movements.
  • Include hedging strategies to protect against currency risks.
  • Regularly review portfolio allocation to ensure balanced exposure.
Risk Factor Impact on the Market Mitigation Strategy
Deflationary pressures Reduced corporate profits Monitor policy changes closely
Geopolitical tensions Sectoral performance dips Diversify across regions/sectors
Currency fluctuations Export profitability loss Employ currency hedging methods

By understanding these risks and preparing accordingly, investors can better position themselves to navigate the complexities of China's stock market.

Strategies for Investors in the Chinese Stock Market

Tactical Approaches to Short-Term Gains

Short-term investors in the Chinese stock market often target rapid price movements, which can be frequent due to the high participation of retail investors. Staying informed about policy announcements, economic data releases, and geopolitical developments is critical for making timely decisions.

Here are some strategies to consider:

  1. Focus on High-Volatility Stocks: Look for sectors like technology or consumer internet companies, which often experience sharp price swings.
  2. Monitor Trading Volumes: High trading volumes in specific stocks can signal upcoming momentum.
  3. Use Stop-Loss Orders: Protect your capital by setting automatic sell triggers to limit losses.

Long-Term Investment Opportunities in China

For those with a longer investment horizon, the Chinese market offers avenues for growth, particularly in emerging sectors. The government's push for innovation and self-reliance has led to significant investment in areas like renewable energy, semiconductors, and artificial intelligence.

Potential long-term opportunities include:

  • Emerging Technologies: Companies involved in AI, electric vehicles, and green energy are likely to benefit from policy support.
  • Consumer Growth: Rising incomes and urbanization continue to drive demand in sectors like e-commerce and healthcare.
  • Infrastructure Development: Ongoing projects in transportation and utilities can provide steady returns.

While the potential for growth is substantial, patience is key. Historical trends show that market rallies in China are often driven by speculative activity rather than sustained earnings growth.

Balancing Risks and Rewards in Portfolio Allocation

Investing in China comes with its own set of risks, from regulatory changes to currency fluctuations. Balancing these risks with potential rewards requires a diversified approach.

A balanced portfolio might include:

Asset Type Suggested Allocation (%)
Chinese Equities 30-40
International Stocks 20-30
Bonds and Fixed Income 20-25
Cash or Alternatives 10-15

Key considerations:

  • Diversify Across Sectors: Avoid over-concentration in volatile industries.
  • Hedge Currency Risk: Consider instruments that protect against yuan depreciation.
  • Stay Updated: Regularly review your portfolio in response to market and policy changes.

By combining tactical short-term strategies with a long-term vision and a balanced portfolio, investors can better navigate the complexities of the Chinese stock market.

Conclusion

Understanding the ups and downs of China's stock market can feel overwhelming, but it's clear that this market operates differently from others. Short-term trends, like daily price momentum, play a big role, and retail investors often drive these changes. While government policies and global economic factors add layers of complexity, they also create opportunities for those who take the time to learn. Whether you're a seasoned investor or just starting out, keeping an eye on these unique patterns can help you make more informed decisions. The key takeaway? Patience and a willingness to adapt are essential when navigating this dynamic market.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Electronic Arts: Potential Growth Inflection In FY26
data.description:
Summary

I am positive on Electronic Arts (NASDAQ:EA). My summarized thesis is that EA could see strong net bookings growth in the coming years, with the inflection point to be potentially in FY26. In particular, the recovery in EA Sports FC engagement, upcoming Battlefield launches, and new content releases like College Football 26 and Battlefield should support growth. In my view, the current valuation at 17x forward PE does not fully reflect this potential upside.

Company overview

EA is one of the world’s largest video game publishers. The company has a lot of famous games under its portfolio, including sports games such as EA Sports FC, Madden NFL, and College Football 26; shooter games such as Battlefield and Apex Legends; and life simulation games like The Sims and Skate. In the past, EA's business relied more on one-off sales to grow the business, but its business model has increasingly shifted toward recurring revenue, with live services now making up ~70% of net bookings.

Earnings results update

Total 3Q25 net bookings came in at $2.22 billion, down 6% y/y, driven by a 3% decline in full-game sales and an 8% decline in live services. Revenue also fell 3% y/y to $1.88 billion. As a percentage of net bookings, the profitability profile was stable, with gross margin coming in at 80%, adj EBIT margin at 41%, and adj net margin at 34%, all of which are the same vs. 3Q24. Although headlines weren’t great, the main takeaway I had was there are clear signs of momentum returning. I discuss these signs of momentum improving below.

EA Sports FC: A Short-Term Setback, but Signs of Recovery Are Clear

A big concern about EA was EA Sports FC, which underperformed in the quarter, which saw mid-single-digit bookings declines due to slower player conversion from FC24 and churn in a competitive player cohort. This is indeed bad news, but I believe the situation is improving. According to management, there are two issues: (1) player conversion delays and (2) engagement churn. Specifically, some players delayed their timeline to upgrade from FC24, while some players in highly competitive segments churned due to gameplay balance issues.

Positively, management responded quickly with a major gameplay update on January 16 and effectively solved these two issues, with tangible results to support this. Since the update: (1) 2 million Ultimate Team players reactivated; (2) FC25 saw record weekly active users since launch; and (3) bookings flipped from negative to positive y/y in the final two weeks of January. In my view, this suggests the issues were temporal rather than structural. In addition, there is also a growth catalyst in FY26 that could drive a strong growth acceleration—the 2026 World Cup.

American Football’s Growth Momentum Could Be an Underappreciated Catalyst

Next, I also think EA’s American football segment deserves more attention. Madden NFL continues to be a highly profitable franchise and is on track to hit >$1 billion in net bookings for FY25. Key operating metrics such as player engagement that was up double digits y/y and Ultimate Team mode continuing to grow point to very strong demand momentum, and this suggests the >$1 billion net bookings target is definitely possible. What’s even more interesting is College Football 26, which launches this summer. For historical context, EA hasn’t released an NCAA football title in over a decade. Given the renewed interest in college football following the playoff expansion, I believe this could be a major new revenue driver. The fan base should also get substantially larger than the past given the inclusion of new participants, and what this means for EA is there is a bigger pool of fans to monetize. Like the Madden NFL series, this NCAA football title could bring in a fresh wave of Ultimate Team players, which opens up a new live services opportunity.

Battlefield, Sims, and Skate Set Up a Broader FY26 Recovery

Putting aside sports, it is also noteworthy that EA broader gaming pipeline is looking strong:

  1. Management confirmed that Battlefield is still scheduled for an FY26 launch.
  2. EA introduced Battlefield Labs (a large-scale player testing program), which I take as management signalling to the market that they are prioritizing quality this time around. This should result in a better quality title that may lead to better engagement rates vs. past Battlefield launches that have not worked out very well
  3. The Sims franchise is also seeing steady bookings growth. Two interesting aspects that could enhance engagement rate are EA new focus on creator-driven content and EA’s Skate reboot.

Of course, nobody knows how well these titles or new launches will perform, but if they work out well, and If Battlefield launches smoothly with College Football 26 seeing strong adoption, it is not hard to imagine EA returning to bookings growth in FY26.

Valuation

I totally understand why the market values EA at just 17x forward PE. The negative net bookings growth, execution issues with past launches (i.e., Battlefield and what happened with EA Sports FC in 3Q25), and the broader weaker macro environment (softer discretionary spending backdrop) may justify this. However, my belief is that the market may be too myopic and not giving any value to a potential turnaround in FY26, which, I think, has a good chance of materializing given the growth catalyst and positive factors I noted above. One way to frame the potential upside is by comparing it to peers. Use Take-Two Interactive Software (TTWO); for instance, the market values the stock at 30x forward PE as consensus expects strong growth acceleration in FY26 (growth expectation 47% y/y). If EA is able to show solid acceleration in net bookings, which should translate into revenue growth acceleration, and depending on how fast growth accelerates, I see a path for EA to trade higher than 17x. For context, 17x is the low end of EA’s trading history. The stock used to trade at ~20x in FY22 when net bookings were in the high teens to low 20s%.

Investment Risk

Regarding EA Sports FC, while management has made the right improvements, the recent incident shows how sensitive players are to the game quality, and if EA screws up a few more times, it may significantly hurt near-term net bookings growth. As for Battlefield, EA’s history of poor Battlefield launches are definitely concerns to be wary about. Any delays or underperformance of the upcoming FY26 release will hurt growth too.

Conclusion

My positive view on EA is based on my belief that the setup for FY26 looks increasingly favorable. EA Sports FC’s recovery is already underway, American football is seeing strong underlying demand, and Battlefield, Sims, and Skate offer new growth opportunities. If execution improves, I see a strong case for growth to accelerate, which could be the catalyst for multiple expansion.

data.title: Dropbox: Low Growth, Fair Valuation And Competition Risks
data.description:
Dropbox(NASDAQ:DBX) is functioning as a mature company in a growing market. This signals that competition is strong, and Dropbox is losing market share. Cloud companies are providing good, bundled solutions that provide more value for each company. In contrast, Dropbox is showing a good feature portfolio and looks fairly valued. Since Dropbox is fairly valued and shows very little earnings per share growth, I rate the stock a hold.

Background

Previously, companies that wished to store data with the ability to access it remotely had to invest large sums in on-premises storage. Now, there are multiple options that allow for online data storage and sharing, creating a unique and updated file that every coworker can access. These modern systems allow for improved collaboration with interfaces for deploying notes and assigning tasks. If the team needs to go back on the file version these tools provide the recent version history.

With the rise of partial and total remote work, more companies are requiring online storage and collaboration solutions generating a large and competitive market for Dropbox.

The document management storage market is estimated to have a size of 8 billion dollars in 2025 and grow at a pace of 17% CAGR by 2032.

Strategy

In this section, I will try to divide the Dropbox strategy into three main bullets and account for the Box (BOX) competition.

  • Allow for improved governance and collaboration while minimizing capex. One of the main impacts Dropbox has in the workplace is improved governance. It is easier for the IT administration to have visibility over the files that are being worked and assign permissions for each user to be able to read-only or write in files. The improved governance and central database also reduce data leakages and intellectual property theft. By not using storage and collaboration solutions like Dropbox, companies were wasting valuable time in sharing documents by email, looking for documents, working on outdated versions and dividing work. By using a centralized storage solution, shadow IT problems are minimized, documents are more visible, people work on the latest versions, have access to the recent historical evolution of versions and have an easier time commenting on the documents and assigning tasks using tools like Dropbox Paper. All these governance and collaboration benefits induced by the online storage service result in no capex to extend on-premises storage.
  • Be the fastest at file sync and large file upload. Latency is a very important component of good digital systems. For example, every year, online retailers lose millions of dollars in revenue due to latency problems in their webpages. It has been proven that quicker digital processes keep humans engaged and amused. Since Dropbox is one of the faster tools in syncing and uploading large files, it contributes to the user likability of the service. However, this effect is more pronounced in companies that share and work with large files, since small files should be quick enough to sync and upload even if the user is working with Box.
  • Use gen AI for improved productivity. Dropbox AI and Dropbox Dash are two features released by Dropbox in 2023. This shows that the company is committed to infusing its platform with gen AI and was a fast mover to convert the gen AI opportunity into revenue growth. Dropbox AI creates summaries of each document and allows the users to ask questions about the document content. The content might be text, code or video. Dropbox Dash integrates with multiple applications such as Microsoft’s and Salesforce’s and enables keyword search across the full integrated environment.

Financials

Comparing the gross margins, both companies have been increasing their gross margins, but Dropbox has consistently beat Box in this department.

Box outpaced Dropbox in the FCF/Employed Capital category showing more efficiency in the business. Both companies are highly efficient in their free cash flow generation.

Considering the debt, Dropbox has been financing buybacks by using debt. The total net debt is around 57% of the free cash flow. In contrast, Box has no net debt.

Box is more valued than Dropbox because of its much higher expected revenue growth and higher efficiency in FCF generation. Box is also a more enterprise friendly partner with more than a thousand integrations with other popular SaaS apps. Thus, this company should have a more solid moat around enterprises where most of the money is made.

Opportunities

Dropbox is not growing revenue or facing very little revenue growth. The company needs to rebalance its structure in order to increase the bottom line via workforce optimization. With more bottom line, there will be more money to invest in buybacks, increasing the stock returns and earnings per share. The company workforce restructuring is a good opportunity to execute that will significantly affect stock returns.

Another opportunity important to point out is the improvement of data governance in the Dash gen AI tool. Dash has the ability to get information from multiple applications and documents, potentially compromising sensitive information. It is important to have a visible dashboard that helps in assigning the users permissions to different applications and documents, improving data governance and protecting sensitive data.

In the Q3 2024 earnings call Dropbox’s management said this:

But what's really resonating with IT leaders is Dash's unique governance capabilities, which we call Protect and Control. For the first time, admins get real-time visibility into everything that's shared in the company across every major content platform from one dashboard with powerful controls to protect sensitive content. Most companies we talk to are doing this manually today, so they've told us they see huge value in automating this and extending these capabilities. And overall, we know this market is attracting significant attention and investment, which both validates the opportunity we've been pursuing while also underscoring the importance of moving quickly to capture it.

Risks

Low historical growth combined with workforce restructuring signal that growth won’t accelerate. In a market that is continuously growing at double digits, we can infer that Dropbox is facing strong competition from its peers and cloud competitors. Growth might contact in the future affecting the stock price.

The company is opting to spend money on buybacks instead of investing in opex. We can consider that Dropbox achieved maturity status in an industry that is growing fast, a major risk for the company.

Valuation

To understand what growth the market is pricing, I like to do a reverse discounted cash flow analysis. To model the discount rate, I use 10%. The initial revenue value is the sell-side analysts estimate for 2025. The starting free cash flow margin is 30%. To generate the terminal multiple, I assumed a terminal multiple of 3 that expands 3% throughout the ten years due to buybacks, resulting in a multiple of 4. Since I’m not doing the math per share and I wish to keep the model simple, I prefer to consider that the buybacks affect the total market cap (They don’t, they just affect the price per share). With the inputs, the growth is balanced to obtain the current market cap.

The model outputs a consistent growth rate of 2.6%. Given the sell-side expectations and the pivot from opex to buybacks, I consider Dropbox slightly overvalued to fairly valued.

Conclusion

Dropbox was one of the first online storage services that revolutionized how companies work. The solution is still very effective for improved governance, collaboration and work processes. The main competitive advantage of Dropbox is its fast syncing compared to Box. The workforce restructuring will improve stock buybacks and increase shareholder returns. The Dash AI governance features are very useful for each company’s IT department, showing some product strength. However, I consider the company fairly valued, and with low earnings per share growth we will see very little returns. I rate Dropbox a hold.

data.title: Americas Gold and Silver: An Undervalued Mid-Tier Producer
data.description:
Introduction

In my opinion, we are getting very close to breaking out in both silver and the silver miners. The HUI has been stuck in a trading range channel since 2013 (see below).

Once silver gets above $35, we should break out of this channel. Gold has been leading, but silver and the miners have lagged. In fact, the margins for silver miners have been awful. We need at least 25% FCF (free cash flow) margins for the silver miners to finally get a bid.

Hecla reported their earnings two days ago, and its share price got smashed down more than 10%. Why did they miss? Poor margins. What’s crazy about silver (and gold) miners is that they don’t have any competition or customers. Instead, they have buyers (plenty of them). This is both a good thing and a bad thing. The bad thing is that they are at the mercy of the silver price. The going spot price of the silver price dictates their margins.

Ironically, if silver was $2 higher last Qtr, then Hecla’s share price would be around $8. As speculators, we can bet that will be the case in the future. And if we are right, then companies like Americas Gold and Silver are going to rip higher from better margins.

Historically, Americas Gold and Silver (NYSE:USAS) (TSX:USA:CA) has been a poor performer and poor executor of hitting targets, and for this reason, many of you are wondering why I’m writing it up. Well, guess what? They recently got a new CEO. He came from Karora and did a great job there. Karora was acquired by Westgold, and the CEO had to find a new job. As I like to say, focus on the future and not the past.

I was a fan of Americas Gold and Silver before the new CEO arrived because of Galena. There are not very many quality silver mines in the world, and Galena is one of them. It’s in an excellent location in Idaho, has high grade, and strong exploration potential. Galena alone is worth 3x their market cap. Why is it so cheap? It’s history. Investors avoid problems.

Stock Name

Symbols

Type

Category

Share Price (US)

FD Shares

FD Mkt Cap (2/15/2025)

Americas Gold and Silver

USAS

Silver

Mid-Tier Producer

54 cents

657M

$356M

Company Overview

Americas Gold and Silver was a mid-tier gold/silver producer in Nevada (Relief Canyon), Mexico (Cosala), and Idaho (Galena). However, they had to put their gold mine (Relief Canyon) on C&M (care & maintenance) because of high costs, and their share price crashed. Today, they are a turnaround story, but a good one.

In 2024, they re-acquired 100% of their flagship Galena silver mine in Idaho. It has a 170M oz silver resource at 400 gpt. Galena should produce 2M oz in 2025 and increase that total for the next few years. I'm expecting the AISC to be around $20, but it will probably be higher. The good news is that costs at Galena are expected to decrease as they increase production. Their Cosala silver property in Mexico should produce about 1 million oz of silver in 2025, with an AISC of around $20 from the offsets. It’s supposed to be a low-cost mine, but they lost money in 2023, and have been trying to get costs lower.

Cosala has a funded EC120 project. This project will use the Cosala mill to add 2.5M oz of AG production for 5 years, with an AISC of $11. Production is scheduled to begin in Q3 2025. This means that in 2026, they could be producing 5M oz of AG.

Note that Cosala has had road blockage problems in the past from protestors, and hopefully, that will not return. But it does add risk.

They have large resources, with about 225 million oz of silver, plus significant base metals (zinc and lead). This should allow them to grow silver production. However, at this time they are not giving guidance for production growth. I expect to see some guidance this year or next.

Their company presentation does not go into detail about the problems at Relief Canyon or when they will be fixed. So, it appears to be serious. Perhaps at $3,000 gold, it will be profitable. It is supposed to produce 70,000 oz a year (their share). The resources are not large (around 700,000 oz), but it has exploration potential on a large property. My guess is that it will be restarted at around 50K oz a year at a $1,500 AISC. They don't have a great balance sheet with about $2 million in cash and about $40 million in debt (a red flag). In 2016, they did a 12 to 1 reverse split. That was painful. Many investors avoid this stock because of its past problems.

Hopefully, all of their bad news is behind them. They lost money in 2022 due to Relief Canyon and again in 2023 from high costs at Cosala and Galena. Their FD market cap crashed from $400 million to $50 million. Ouch. It’s now back to $350 million, but there was a lot of share dilution since 2023.If you don't mind their historical baggage, they could be a highflyer if silver prices rise. They have huge leverage to silver prices. Plus, they might restart Relief Canyon at some point. Also, Galena is likely to increase production significantly with its large resources.

Company Info

Cash: $2M

Debt: $42M

Current Silver Resources: 200M oz AG at 300 gpt.

Estimated Future Silver Resources: 150M oz AG at 300 gpt.

Estimated Future Silver Production: 6M oz AG.

Estimated Future Silver All-in Costs (breakeven): $28 per oz.

Current Gold Resources: 700K oz AU at .7 gpt.

Estimated Future Gold Resources: 700K oz AU at .7 gpt.

Estimated Future Gold Production: 5K oz AU.

Estimated Future Gold All-in Costs (breakeven): $2,000 per oz.

Note: My future timeline is 3-4 years.

Scorecard (1 to 10)

Properties/Projects: 8

Costs/Grade/Economics: 7.5

People/Management: 7.5

Cash/Debt: 6.5

Location Risk: 8

Risk-Reward: 7.5

Upside Potential: 8

Production Growth Potential/Exploration: 8

Overall Rating: 7.5

Notes: The scale is 1 to 10, with 10 being best. This is just a general overview of the company, looking for strengths and weaknesses.

8.5: Excellent.

8: Very Good.

7.5: Almost Good.

7: Satisfactory.

6.5: Marginal.

6: Weak.

Strengths/Positives

Significant upside potential.

Strong production growth potential.

Good mine location (mining district).

Strong management.

Strong insiders.

High leverage to higher silver prices.

Exploration potential.

Note: These are my opinion and a general overview of their strengths/weaknesses.

Risks/Red Flags

Likely some share dilution.

Potential lower gold/silver prices.

Weak balance sheet.

Speculation stock (unexpected events).

Assuming higher gold/silver prices.

Long-term investment is needed for them to grow production.

Note: These are my opinion and a general overview of their risks.

Valuation ($4,000 gold prices and $80 silver prices)

Silver production estimate for the long term: 6M oz.

All-In Costs (break-even): $28 per oz.

6M oz. x ($80 - $28) = $312 million annual FCF.

Gold production estimate for the long term: 50K oz.

All-In Costs (break-even): $2,000 per oz.

50K oz. x ($4,000 - $2,000) = $100 million annual FCF.

Total FCF = $312 + $100 = $432 million.

$432 million x 15 (multiplier) = $6.5 billion.

Current FD market cap: $356 million.

Upside potential: 1,700%.

Note: This valuation is for 2028 and assumes they can reach 6M oz AG production at $28 per/oz break-even costs per oz (FCF). Plus, 50K oz AU at $2,000 per/oz break-even costs.

Note: I used $4,000 gold prices to identify their future value because I am a long-term investor who plans to wait for higher gold prices. While $4,000 might seem too high as a future price, I expect gold to reach $3,000 in 2025. If we use a GSR (gold-silver ratio) of 50, then we get $80 silver price. The GSR was 39 in 2011, so a 50 seems conservative.

Note: My All-In Costs are the expected costs that will generate FCF.

Note: I used a future FCF multiplier of 15 because I do not expect significant share dilution. I could have used a lower multiple to account for dilution and risk. Because we are projecting out 3 years into the future, this estimate is only a best guess.

Balance Sheet/Share Dilution

We can expect some share dilution because of their weak balance sheet, which has very little cash. Plus, they may need to raise money to restart Relief Canyon in Nevada, or expand production at Galena. I’m hoping they can borrow what they need.

Risk/Reward

The risk-reward is very good, in my opinion, because of all the Galena mine, which has a lot of high grade silver. Other than the balance sheet risk and their current low margins, they seem well positioned to benefit from higher silver prices.

In addition to owning a large, long-life, high-grade silver mine, the other thing that makes this company appealing is the location of the Galena mine. It’s in Idaho. There are very few large silver mines in safe locations now that Mexico has become no longer reliable as a mining-friendly country.

Investment Thesis

(Note: The section can be redundant in some of my articles because I tend to repeat some ideas).

I always say that you can't pick winners, and that all you can do is pick potential winners. You never know which of your miners are going to do well. I've learned that from experience. The ones that do well tend to surprise you.

Americas Gold and Silver is a strong potential winner because of Galena, potential high FCF at higher silver prices, exploration potential, good location, strong insiders, a good management team, and a current low valuation.

I think they could easily generate more FCF than their current FD mkt cap. This won’t happen in 2025 or 2026, but potentially in 2027 or 2028. This is a long-term bet where we give them time to expand production at Galena and restart Relief Canyon.

Final Thoughts

If you are a believer like I am that gold is going to $4,000 (pushing silver above $75), then Americas Gold and Silver is a pretty good bet. These are the types of high-alpha stocks that I look for. I like to find undervalued producer that are expecting to grow production and lower their costs.

I also like the management team, with its new proven CEO, which, I think, can expand production significantly and execute much better than the previous team.

The key to the upside is going to be gold and silver prices. Also, we don’t want them to dilute shares severely. Plus, we want them to expand production significantly.

I don’t know what’s going to happen to the valuation of silver producers for the rest of this decade if silver prices explode, but my guess is that they will be a lot more valuable than the current valuations. My guess is that FCF multiples are going to explode higher. I don’t think it’s crazy to think that Americas Gold and Silver could reach a 20.

I always look for 5-baggers with 10-bagger potential. Americas Gold and Silver fits that thesis. However, don’t expect it to be a slam dunk. Never over-allocate to a stock because you like it a lot and think it is a slam dunk. However, this is probably one you don’t want to under-allocate. Best guess!

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Coca-Cola Europacific Partners: Rating Downgrade On High Valuation And Visible Headwinds
data.description:
Investment action

I recommended a buy rating for Coca-Cola Europacific Partners (NASDAQ:CCEP) when I wrote about it last year in March, as I expected a positive growth outlook for FY24, especially with the inclusion of the Philippines business unit. Based on my current outlook and analysis, I recommend a hold rating. My key update is that while CCEP continues to execute well, the ongoing volume decline in Western Europe and uncertainties in Indonesia make it difficult to justify further upside. Additionally, with the stock trading at a valuation premium to peers, I believe the risk-reward is now less attractive.

Review

CCEP reported earnings on 14th Feb, with revenue coming in at EUR 20.7 billion, a 3.5% y/y growth on an FX-neutral basis. Revenue performance was slightly below consensus expectations, primarily due to continued volume weakness in Western Europe. However, it was good to see that CCEP was able to drive a 2.6% y/y increase in revenue per unit case. Comparable EBIT grew 8% y/y, helped by cost-saving initiatives that offset cost inflation. In terms of free cash flow, CCEP generated EUR 1.8 billion in free cash flow [FCF] for FY24, which is amazing considering that CCEP invested EUR 1 billion back into the business. For FY25, management reaffirmed its outlook, which includes FX-neutral revenue growth of ~4% and EBIT growth of ~7%.

I walked away from CCEP earnings with a balanced view compared to my bullish view previously. On the positive side of things, firstly, I really like CCEP’s execution in growing pricing without significant volume loss. Take 4Q24, for instance; despite a rather difficult consumer spending backdrop (which caused negative volume growth in 4Q24), revenue per unit case still grew 2.6%. This shows that CCEP’s revenue growth management [RGM] – price-pack architecture, promotions, product mix management, etc. – is working.

Secondly, the Philippines continues to be an attractive growth region, which CCEP saw double-digit volume growth in FY24. Notably, CCEP gained ~300 bps of value share in sparkling and now holds about 50% of the NARTD (non-alcohol ready to drink) category. If you recall my 2Q23 earnings update for CCEP, I pointed out that this region is a fast-growing market with a large total addressable market. With CCEP owning a sizeable market share, I remain confident in CCEP's ability to tap into this market’s long-term growth potential—driven by a young, growing population and increasing per capita consumption. It was also great to hear management stepping on the acceleration pedal as they accelerate capital investments, pulling forward new production lines and distribution infrastructure. This investment should help sustain high single-digit growth in the Philippines and offset some of the weaker volume trends in Western Europe.

Lastly, there is a good chance that CCEP gets included in the FTSE 100, which could provide a near-term technical catalyst, as passive funds tracking the index may increase their exposure to the stock. While this does not change CCEP’s fundamentals, it could support share price performance in the near term.

On the negative side of things, my major concern is that volume growth in Western Europe has not meaningfully recovered. Away-from-home demand declined 3.9% y/y in 4Q24, and while CCEP has managed to offset this through pricing, the trend of volume decline is still a headwind. Both away-from-home and at-home volumes were also down at 0.9% and 1.1% y/y, respectively. Again, while pricing has offset this decline, there is only so much that CCEP can raise prices, especially given the poor consumer spending environment in Europe.

Likewise, in Indonesia, the outlook is uncertain. It was so bad that CCEP had to take an EUR 175 million impairment charge related to its Indonesian operations, which is evidence that underlying demand is weaker than expected. While management remains committed to the market, there is little that management can do to resolve geopolitical factors (boycott of certain brands). Granted that this could be temporary issues and that CCEP continues to invest in improving its route-to-market strategy, the timing of a full recovery remains uncertain.

Valuation limits upside potential

Lastly, now that CCEP trades at ~19x forward PE (was 16x when I wrote in March last year), which is near its 10-year high, the upside seems limited as well. When compared to other non-alcoholic beverage players, CCEP actually seems overvalued for its growth and margin profile. The industry is expected to grow at mid-single digits for the next two years with an average net margin of ~9%; this compares similarly to CCEP, but CCEP trades at ~4x the premium to the industry. I don’t think this premium is justified, especially given the visible headwinds I noted above. But I do recognize that the technical catalyst may be supporting the share price here, which has nothing to do with CCEP’s fundamentals. As such, I am only downgrading to hold and not a sell.

Final thoughts

My recommendation is a hold. CCEP remains a well-managed company with strong execution and an attractive long-term opportunity in the Philippines. However, I have concerns about the weak volume trends in Europe and the geopolitical headwinds in Indonesia. CCEP’s valuation also appears stretched relative to peers, limiting upside. While a potential FTSE 100 inclusion could provide a short-term technical boost, I prefer to wait for more clarity on volume recovery before turning bullish again.

data.title: Apple: Has One Of The Biggest Opportunities In The World Ahead Of It
data.description:
The Apple Investment Thesis

After Apple Inc. (NASDAQ:AAPL) relentlessly outperformed the S&P 500 (SPY) over the past 10 years, as we can see in the chart above, I expect the next 10 years to be similar or even better. Because Apple has one of the biggest opportunities in front of it, simply because it has an incredible number of Apple devices in circulation that can be leveraged as the health ecosystem matures. In fact, I think the health ecosystem could be as successful for Apple as the cloud was and is for Amazon (AMZN).

Apple Health and Apple Health Monitoring

Apple has one of the greatest opportunities of our time on its hands. The large number of Apple ecosystem devices in circulation gives Apple a hard-to-combat competitive advantage. The 2.2 billion Apple devices currently in use are simply something that has to be earned over years of high-quality products, services, and an ecosystem that works almost without interruption. And if Apple can capitalize on this huge share of devices with health monitoring and life-enhancing services, it will be an even bigger success than the iPhone.

On the one hand, this will increase the service share of sales, which is the high-margin business, which will have a positive effect on earnings, and on the other hand, Apple already has the perfect customer thanks to its premium positioning. Apple customers have no problem spending money, as evidenced by the ever-increasing prices of iPhones, and affluent customers tend to be more willing to invest in their health. These are ideal conditions for Apple.

In addition, Apple's design department is skilled at creating products that are trendy and timely. I mean, how many younger people who have hearing problems don't wear hearing aids because they don't think it's cool. But now you can wear AirPods Pro 2 with hearing aid functionality and no one would know you were using them for that functionality because the product is already so socially embedded.

And I think in the future we'll see other things that are picked up by our iPhones, AirPods, or Apple watches that will give us real-time data that we can either have doctors or AI analyze. For example, I would not be surprised if in 5 years Apple devices are more powerful in some areas of monitoring than many second-tier hospitals around the world are today.

Apple is known for making its products as user-friendly and intuitive as possible, and that is where I think many medical device companies fail today. Anyone who has ever had one of those clunky and expensive sleep monitors at home probably knows what I mean. And with Apple's cash position and excellent employees, I could see Apple disrupting many medical areas, either through its own R&D or through M&A, and then improving the design of the components.

Butterfly iQ, for example, is a mobile phone-based ultrasound solution that is already being used in many practices to replace old, bulky equipment. It is quicker to set up and can even be used outside the office in an emergency. A win-win for patients and medical staff. And I fully expect that Apple's ecosystem will continue to expand to include many medical applications and devices that will make life easier and safer for many people.

And most importantly, Apple is committed to protecting privacy to the fullest extent possible, so customers can rest assured that their sensitive health information is safe.

Apple's Programming Kits

With APIs such as HealthKit, ResearchKit, CareKit and SensorKit, Apple makes it easy for companies to place their apps in the Apple ecosystem and benefit from Apple's cutting-edge technology. The result is applications that provide significant value, such as StrivePD, which collects data through Apple's Movement Disorder API to provide the best care for Parkinson's patients.

In other words, the better the sensors, the better the apps, which in turn means that customers are more and more integrated into the Apple ecosystem, which drives up the cost of switching.

Apple's latest revenue figures

Product figures were roughly flat year-over-year, which is not too bad because first, the amount of equipment in circulation is already massive, and second, the service business has much better margins anyway.

And if we look at the gross margins, we see that Products is about 39%, while Services is almost twice as high at 75%. And since the Services segment is growing much faster, the gross margin should continue to grow.

The iPhone product line generated lower revenue in the quarter compared to the year-ago quarter, partially offset by higher Mac and iPad revenue. Much more important, however, is the fact that service revenues continue to grow at a double-digit rate (~14%). Because as long as they grow faster than the product category, this leads to better margins in the long term. And because I think Apple is going to be one of the major players in the health services space, I think Apple is going to see a lot of margin expansion going forward.

Therefore, I do not consider the declining sales in the Chinese market, which have caused some concern, to be that important for the time being. This is because they will increase again as soon as Apple gets going in the health sector. As I do not believe that the combination of the devices in circulation and Apple's design and usability expertise can be beaten.

Apple's ROIC + Capital Allocation

In addition, Apple is one of the few companies that has been able to improve its already astonishingly high ROIC. For example, the low for the last 5 years was 28%, which would have been a fantastic result for over 95% of companies worldwide, but even that has been beaten and ROIC is now 56% with a 5-year average of 49%. Therefore, it should come as no surprise that the WACC is significantly beaten and that Apple has a strongly positive ROIC-WACC spread.

  • ROIC: 56.16%
  • WACC: 8.4%
  • ROIC-WACC Spread: 56.16% - 8.4% = 47.76%

And in addition to its fantastic ROIC, Apple is also very shareholder-friendly, as evidenced by its steadily declining share count and steadily increasing dividend. This, coupled with increasing revenues, is a recipe for great total shareholder returns.

Apple's Balance Sheet

The fact that Apple also has a great balance sheet is the icing on the cake. In fact, the $53 billion in cash and cash equivalents exceeds the $10 billion in current portion of LT debt by a multiple.

And Apple's times earned interest is 3732x, while the average company in the S&P 500 has only about 10x interest coverage. So the incredible quality of Apple is not just in its capital allocation and return on capital, but also in its interest coverage and balance sheet. In fact, interest coverage or times interest earned clearly shows that Apple's liquidity and financial strength are much better than the financial health of the average company. So Apple is exceptionally well positioned, and its balance sheet shows no major weaknesses in terms of future liquidity or strength.

With not much debt coming due in the next few years, as we can see in the chart above, the cash and cash equivalents can be used for investments. And here I expect that this cash will be used for M&A activities in the healthcare sector as interesting opportunities arise.

If we look at Apple's M&A history, we see that in recent years there have been 2-3 transactions per year. In 2024, these were DarwinAI in the AI sector and Pixelmator, a software for digital art and photography. In health, Apple acquired Tueo Health, an asthma monitoring company, in 2018, and Beddit, a sleep monitoring hardware company, in 2017, and is using them on the Apple Watch and iOS.

That's why I think we're going to see about 2 to 3 company acquisitions again in 2025, and I think the focus will be on AI and health. With many AI companies also likely to bring further advancements to the Apple Health ecosystem through their models.

Risks

I mean, the obvious risks are that Apple will not be the winner in healthcare and that other companies will manage to attack Apple's moat. Because If Apple is no longer seen as a status symbol, which, I think, is unrealistic at the moment, then sales would definitely take a hit. But I still often see people look at you with disdain if you don't have an iPhone, so I think that moat is still in place.

Other smartphone manufacturers have a similar number of devices in use and have also built an ecosystem, like Samsung, for example. But Apple still has the advantage that its customer base, in my opinion, is much less sensitive to price increases or high prices because Apple is perceived as a premium manufacturer. So Apple will probably be able to charge a premium for health applications.

I mean, if Apple can capture just 10% of the current 2.2 billion Apple devices and charge them $9 a month, that would be 220 million x $9 = $1.98 billion in additional revenue each month, or just under $24 billion a year in additional high-margin revenue. And this is just a small part of the potential I see. In fact, I think there will be a number of applications and devices that will make the ecosystem something huge.

Valuation

Apple is currently trading at 38x, which is above the 5-year average of 29x. But I think this is justified because, first, Apple is likely to benefit from margin expansion as the share of services grows. Second, Apple has stayed out of the AI arms race and will likely be more cost-efficient than many of its peers, and third, Apple is simply a better company today than it was five years ago. And Apple simply has incredible opportunities that justify a premium to the 5-year average.

If we take a look at the projected cap ex spending, we can see that the megacaps plan to spend more than $300 billion in 2025. Meta (META), Alphabet (GOOG), Microsoft, and Amazon alone plan to make $320 billion in data center and AI CapEx investments, while Apple's strategy currently looks more like renting capacity from cloud providers than investing heavily in infrastructure itself. That is why I am assuming at the moment that Apple will be more cost-efficient in the field of AI.

Based on the fact that there are more Apple devices in circulation today than there were five years ago, and that the iPhones, the Apple Watches, and the ecosystem have all evolved, I think Apple is stronger than it was five years ago. The bigger the ecosystem, the more customers are locked in. As a result, the switching costs are also high.

And since I believe that the healthcare ecosystem will start to generate revenue growth in the next few years, I think the analyst estimates above are too low. Especially given the fact that service revenue has much higher margins. I think AI will bring a lot of interesting apps and applications to the App Store and the ecosystem that will be high margin for Apple.

Assuming that the multiple in 5 years is the same as today, therefore 38x, the analyst estimates for FY29 EPS of $13.96 would result in a share price of ~$530. This alone would represent an upside of more than 100% from today's share price of ~$244.

However, if we compare the actual EPS to the analyst estimates, we see that there has been a beat every time since 2013, and the analysts have underestimated Apple's strength. And I think that will continue. Therefore, I think it is very likely that FY29 EPS will be above estimates and EPS in the $15+ range is realistic.

Conclusion

The opportunity presented by the 2.2 billion devices in circulation is simply gigantic. If Apple can build an ecosystem in health monitoring or health that is as high quality as the current one, I think my estimate is very conservative. Because then Apple can probably win much more than 10% of the devices for this product. And then there are the numerous up-selling and cross-selling opportunities. So the potential is just huge. And that is why I see a lot of potential for Apple in the long term.

data.title: Lower Levered Ares Capital Still Generates More Than Enough
data.description:
With each report, Ares Capital (NASDAQ:ARCC) demonstrated an ability to outperform its payouts. Once again, the February report for December proved that thesis true. Management continues with a constant dividend in spite of stellar earnings. With this business development company (BDC), two performance factors drive current and future performances, leverage and investment rates of return. With Ares, the rate of return highly correlates with Fed overnight interest rates. The two track each other. With a neutral or accommodating stance now in vogue, the question that investors ask, can earnings continue high enough to pay the long-time $0.48 per quarter dividend? Even more important, will management ever decide to increase the dividend? When is enough? Well, we continue our coverage, the last Predicting Ares Capital's Performance predicted results at a 3.5% Fed Interest Rate target. We outlined puts and takes. That quest continues.

4th Quarter

We begin with the quarter results summaries in the next slide.

A few other critical results follow: core earnings and Net Investment Income each equaled $0.55 while leverage trended flat at approximately 1.00 net cash considerably lower than the target of 1.20.

Of other interest, LTM EBITDA of portfolio companies grew 11% up from 10% in the 3rd quarter and 9% in the final quarter of 23. We believe that this comment by management reflects and conveys an important practice of the company.

"In 2024, over 70% of our new commitments were to existing borrowers, and importantly, we are increasingly being asked to provide a larger portion of our borrowers' overall capital structures. As an illustration, our share of the overall financings for our top 10 largest incumbent borrowers more than doubled in each of the past three quarters."

Non-accrual rates ticked up slightly to 1.7% up 40 basis points yet, still significantly under long-term averages of 2.8%.

Management noted that the lower earnings came from, "Our decline in core earnings was largely driven by the impact of the decline in yields from the portfolio as base rates at the end of the year were nearly 100 basis points lower than where they were at the end of 2023."

In terms of our leverage, the company ended the fourth quarter with debt-to-equity ratio, net of available cash, of 0.99 times, down from the 1.03 a quarter ago. Management added, "We believe our significant amount of dry powder positions us well to continue supporting our existing portfolio company commitments as well as new investing activities."

History Table of Key Performance

Our factors table from our last article is listed next slightly modified follows.

Key Performance

Of most importance for investors, the Weighted Average Yield fail approximately 1.2% while the Fed rates dropped roughly the same.

Marketplace

Management discussed the marketplace experience for 2024 labeling it strong. With the low leverage, they noted the ability to put a lot of capital to work going into the future. Then, they added this interesting statistic,

"During 2024, we reviewed a record volume of new opportunities totaling more than $650 billion, and the number of potential investments we evaluated during the year grew each quarter on a year-over-year basis. Given the strength of our pipeline, we were also able to generate a record level of new commitments, net of repayments, which totaled $5 billion for the year."

With its largest publicly traded BDC, management claimed an advantage in a very competitive market.

They reminded the analysts that they have the ability to compete in the lower, middle and upper middle marketplace. Within the lower and middle markets, deals carry a 25 to 50 basis point advantage.

When asked about spreads going forward by Mark Hughes of Truist Securities, Kipp deVeer, CEO, opined:

"I think most of the decrease we saw pretty ratably through last year. 100, 150 basis points has been the number that we quoted elsewhere in terms of the declines that we've seen, whether it was repricing existing names or new deals. For the time being, I feel like it's pretty much plateaued again because we play across the entire spectrum, smaller companies, larger companies."

Ares is seeing the marketplace stabilize in the past few months.

Dividend

Next, a subject of the most importance, the dividend, deserves a discussion. When asked, DeVeer, noted that they had had a long discussion about a special and for many reasons decided against one. He was very coy with his answer, no detail. He added that this has to wait until next year.

Included within this subject was discussion about lifting leverage. He stated,

"Yeah, I think we'd like the leverage ratio to be higher. Being able to increase the leverage ratio is obviously a driver of earnings, which I think will be important if and when rates continue to kind of come down, right? That's one of the countervailing levers that we can pull to drive earnings in the face of tighter spreads and lower rates. So, we're fortunate in that we're still materially outearning the core dividend so we don't feel a desperate need to do that. But again, just a reminder, that's a lever that we can and will pull."

For the first time in our coverage, management admitted that this is a critical parameter for increasing yields with or when rates fall once again. Now, we have to ask, what do they see? Why isn't the leverage increasing? Is it possible that there aren't enough good opportunities to cover the need for this very large investment? They reviewed $600 billion opportunities and committed to $5 billion. We aren't sure. But this is the most important area to monitor. For now, they earn plenty to pay the dividend and more.

The company still carries forward a lot of undistributed income. From the 10-Q, "Ares Capital estimates that it will carry forward excess taxable income of approximately $922 million or $1.37."

Risk

BDCs face recession risks like any other investment. One might be coming. Ed Dodd, a former very successful data miner with Wall Street, wrote a column on the probability of a recession. Within his comments, he included at least two gems,

“We are seeing a recession in 2025. The rest of the globe is already starting to roll over. It’s going to be a worldwide recession. There is going to be a mini housing crisis."

And,

"So, we are going to have a recession . . . Then, Trump gets his policies, and he has a very short window of opportunity to get all of his policies enacted. If he does, we will be booming on the other side of this.”

For investors, Ares does heavily consider downside risks. From the call,

"As a sign of additional strength in our portfolio, at the end of the fourth quarter, our weighted average loan to value was 44%, which we believe provides us with strong downside protection . . ."

The story is certainly mixed. From commentary of the latest inflation report,

"January's hot inflation report coupled with the stellar monthly jobs report last week delivered a mighty blow to rate-cut expectations, with some on Wall Street reiterating calls for the Federal Reserve interest rates to remain on a long hold for the year."

With rates likely on hold, what seems clear, Ares Capital earns more than enough to pay the dividend. Whether to buy, sell or hold depends on stock price. Unless we see efforts to increase leverage, stock prices near $23 suggest a hold rating. Thus, we have a hold on the stock. We own a lot of shares and don't plan to sell anytime soon. We just believe that after the dividend is paid in six weeks, a cheaper price will come. The lower leverage posture won't in our view affect the dividend anytime soon but just don't get a mindset for pay raises anytime soon. And if a recession hits, the conservative posture will provide protection. We, also, must wonder, can Ares find enough solid investments for increasing leverage? To find $5 billion last year, management gleaned through $600 billion. For the needed increase, the gleaning now requires almost a trillion, yes, a trillion. But enough is enough.
